content.text: Release Train Scope and Cut-Line Process
The
cut-line

is the moment in each release train where scope becomes final. Before the cut, features flow in freely; after it, only critical bug-fixes are permitted on the release branch. Managing what crosses the cut-line — and what defers to the next train — is one of the most consequential engineering-process decisions a team makes.
The cut-line is not a failure to ship a feature; it is a deliberate choice to protect train quality. A deferred feature ships two weeks later. A broken train ships with defects forever.
This sub-subtopic covers how to define scope commitments at train kick-off, how to run a disciplined cut-line meeting, and how to automate the labelling and enforcement mechanics in GitHub.

attrs.alt: Cut-line decision flow: risk assessment, auto-approve vs TL review, deferred to next train

content.text: Scope Commitment at Kick-Off
Scope commitment is the output of the first ceremony in a train cycle. It answers three questions for every candidate feature:
Is it ready to be worked on?
— design is approved, dependencies are resolved, and the work has been broken into small-enough PRs
Can it realistically ship in this window?
— estimated effort fits the available capacity with margin for review and testing
What happens if it's not done?
— the 'skip' scenario must be acceptable; if a feature is a hard dependency for something else, both items share that risk

Features that fail any of these gates go on the next train's candidate list rather than entering current scope. This filters scope before the sprint begins rather than discovering overcommitment mid-train.
Scope status
Meaning
GitHub label
committed
Will ship in this train barring a blocker
train-committed
stretch
Ships if capacity allows; lowest cut priority
train-stretch
deferred
Explicitly not in this train
train-deferred
blocked
Waiting on external dependency
train-blocked
Risk Scoring at Kick-Off
Every committed feature gets a risk score that drives how much scrutiny it receives at the cut-line. Score on two axes:
Axis
Low (1)
Medium (2)
High (3)
Blast radius
Single endpoint or UI element
Multiple services or shared library
Core model integration or auth path
Test coverage
Full unit + integration coverage
Partial coverage, manual smoke test
No automated tests yet
Reversibility
Feature flag, instant rollback
Partial flag, data migration rollback
Data schema change, no flag
Sum the axis scores: 3–4 = low risk (auto-approve at cut-line), 5–6 = medium (PM sign-off), 7–9 = high (TL review required, consider deferral).
The Cut-Line Meeting
The cut-line meeting happens on Thursday of week 2 (day 11 of a 14-day train). It is time-boxed to 30 minutes. The agenda is fixed:
TL reads the committed scope list and current status (green / amber / red) — 10 min
Any amber or red item's owner states blockers and gives a confidence level (High / Medium / Low) — 10 min
PM and TL decide: in, defer, or convert to stretch — 10 min
Decision rules that prevent argument loops:
Any item rated Low confidence
is automatically deferred unless TL judges the risk acceptable in writing (a Slack message is sufficient audit trail)
High-risk items with no feature flag
are deferred unless a rollback plan exists and is documented in the runbook
Stretch items
are cut by default if any committed item is amber — stretch scope consumes capacity needed to clear blockers
Automating the Cut-Line in GitHub
After the meeting, run the cut-line script to mechanically enforce the decisions. The script reads a YAML decision file produced by the PM and applies labels:

# train-42-cut-line.yml  (committed to repo by PM post-meeting)
train: 42
freeze_sha: ""   # filled in by cut-line script
decisions:
  - pr: 1201
    status: in
    risk: low
    flag: cache_streaming
  - pr: 1198
    status: in
    risk: high
    flag: model_upgrade
    rollback_runbook: docs/runbooks/model-rollback.md
  - pr: 1195
    status: deferred
    reason: "Low confidence from owner; ships Train 43"
  - pr: 1187
    status: stretch

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""apply_cut_line.py — label PRs and record freeze SHA."""
import os, sys, subprocess
import yaml, requests

REPO  = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
TOKEN = os.environ["GH_TOKEN"]
H = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"}
API = "https://api.github.com"

def set_labels(pr_number: int, labels: list[str]) -> None:
    # Remove old train labels first
    existing = requests.get(f"{API}/repos/{REPO}/issues/{pr_number}/labels", headers=H).json()
    for lbl in existing:
        if lbl["name"].startswith("train-"):
            requests.delete(f"{API}/repos/{REPO}/issues/{pr_number}/labels/{lbl['name']}", headers=H)
    requests.post(f"{API}/repos/{REPO}/issues/{pr_number}/labels", headers=H, json={"labels": labels})

def freeze_sha(branch: str) -> str:
    r = requests.get(f"{API}/repos/{REPO}/git/ref/heads/{branch}", headers=H).json()
    return r["object"]["sha"]

def main() -> None:
    with open(sys.argv[1]) as f:
        cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)

    train = cfg["train"]
    branch = f"release/train-{train}"
    sha = freeze_sha(branch)
    cfg["freeze_sha"] = sha

    for item in cfg["decisions"]:
        pr = item["pr"]
        status = item["status"]
        risk = item.get("risk", "low")
        labels = [f"train-{status}"]
        if risk == "high":
            labels.append("requires-rollback-plan")
        set_labels(pr, labels)
        print(f"PR #{pr}: {status} ({risk} risk)")

    # Write back freeze SHA
    with open(sys.argv[1], "w") as f:
        yaml.dump(cfg, f, default_flow_style=False)
    print(f"Train {train} freeze SHA: {sha[:12]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Feature Flags as a Cut-Line Safety Net
Every in-train feature that carries medium or high risk must ship behind a
feature flag
— a boolean config value that defaults to
off
in production. This means the code is deployed but the behaviour is inert, giving the option to enable gradually (canary) or roll back by config change rather than a full redeploy.

# src/config.py
import os

# Feature flags — all default to False in production.
# Enable via environment variable for canary / staged rollout.
FEATURE_CACHE_STREAMING  = os.getenv("FEATURE_CACHE_STREAMING",  "false").lower() == "true"
FEATURE_MODEL_UPGRADE    = os.getenv("FEATURE_MODEL_UPGRADE",    "false").lower() == "true"
FEATURE_TOKEN_DASHBOARD  = os.getenv("FEATURE_TOKEN_DASHBOARD",  "false").lower() == "true"
Flags are the primary mechanism that allows high-risk items to cross the cut-line. If a flag-guarded feature proves problematic post-deploy, the rollback is a config update with no downtime:

# Disable a feature flag in Kubernetes via config update — no code deploy needed
kubectl set env deployment/claude-api FEATURE_CACHE_STREAMING=false

# Verify rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/claude-api
kubectl rollout history deployment/claude-api
Handling Post-Freeze Hotfixes
After the cut-line, only two categories of change are permitted on the release branch:
P1 / P2 bug fixes
— regressions introduced by the current train's code that would block the deploy or cause user-visible breakage
Security patches
— CVEs in dependencies or discovered vulnerabilities in the current train's changes
Everything else — new features, performance improvements, refactors — waits for the next train. The merge process for a hotfix requires explicit TL approval captured as a PR comment:

# .github/workflows/freeze-guard.yml
# Blocks merges to release/* branches unless labelled 'hotfix-approved'
name: Freeze Guard

on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - 'release/**'

jobs:
  check-freeze-label:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Require hotfix-approved label
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          LABELS=$(gh pr view ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --json labels -q '.labels[].name' | tr '\n' ' ')
          if ! echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "hotfix-approved"; then
            echo "ERROR: Merges to release branches require the 'hotfix-approved' label."
            echo "A tech lead must add this label after reviewing the fix."
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Hotfix label present — merge permitted."
Deferral Communication

When a feature is deferred, the communication to stakeholders must be immediate and specific. Ambiguous deferral messages ('it might slip') are worse than early definitive ones ('it ships Train 43, deploy target 2025-08-26'). Use a template:

**Feature deferral notice — PR #1195**

*Feature:* Retry budget telemetry
*Deferred from:* Train 42 (deploy 2025-08-12)
*Ships on:* Train 43 (deploy target 2025-08-26)

*Reason:* Owner confidence was Low at cut-line — insufficient test coverage
for the aggregation path. Deferral avoids a P2 post-deploy investigation.

*Impact:* No user-visible change. Internal dashboards will not show retry
budget data for this release cycle.

*Action required:* @carol to complete integration tests by Train 43 kick-off (2025-08-13).
Tracking Scope Drift Over Trains

Scope drift — the gap between what was committed and what shipped — compounds silently unless tracked per-train. A lightweight Python script queries the GitHub Milestones API after each deploy and writes a JSON trend file:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""scope_drift.py — append per-train metrics to trend.json."""
import os, json, datetime, requests

REPO  = os.environ["GITHUB_REPOSITORY"]
TOKEN = os.environ["GH_TOKEN"]
H = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"}
API = "https://api.github.com"
TREND_FILE = "metrics/scope-drift.json"

def milestone_metrics(number: int) -> dict:
    ms = requests.get(f"{API}/repos/{REPO}/milestones/{number}", headers=H).json()
    opened = ms["open_issues"]
    closed = ms["closed_issues"]
    total  = opened + closed
    return {
        "train":          ms["title"],
        "committed":      total,
        "shipped":        closed,
        "deferred":       opened,
        "scope_accuracy": round(closed / total, 3) if total else 0,
        "recorded_at":    datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
    }

def append_trend(metrics: dict) -> None:
    try:
        with open(TREND_FILE) as f:
            trend = json.load(f)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        trend = []
    # Deduplicate by train name
    trend = [t for t in trend if t["train"] != metrics["train"]]
    trend.append(metrics)
    os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(TREND_FILE), exist_ok=True)
    with open(TREND_FILE, "w") as f:
        json.dump(trend, f, indent=2)
    print(f"Recorded: {metrics}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    milestone_number = int(sys.argv[1])
    m = milestone_metrics(milestone_number)
    append_trend(m)
Run this script as a post-deploy step in the release workflow. After several trains, the trend file shows whether scope accuracy is improving:

Consistent scope accuracy below 85% is a signal to reduce committed scope at kick-off — teams are over-committing rather than the work being unexpectedly complex. Consistent accuracy above 98% signals under-commitment — the team could take on more stretch items without risk.
Summary
Scope commitment
at kick-off answers three questions: ready to work on, fits capacity, acceptable to skip
Risk scoring
on blast radius, test coverage, and reversibility determines cut-line scrutiny level (3 axes, each 1–3, sum drives tier)
Cut-line meeting
is 30 minutes, time-boxed, with fixed agenda and explicit decision rules that prevent scope creep
YAML decision file
records cut-line decisions; automated script applies GitHub labels and records the freeze SHA
Feature flags
are mandatory for medium/high-risk in-train features — they decouple deploy from activation and enable instant rollback
Post-freeze hotfixes
require 'hotfix-approved' label enforced by CI; new features always wait for the next train
Deferral notices
are sent immediately with the specific target train date — ambiguity is more disruptive than early confirmation
